title: Computing Homology Cycles with Certified Geometry
speaker: Tamal Dey

proof_stages.label: Optimization problems for certified homology cycles
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates feature localization, fixes mostly Z/2 coefficients, distinguishes computing homology ranks from computing geometrically good generators, defines weighted smallest bases, and states three target results: optimal H1 bases, point-cloud approximation, and optimal homologous cycles.

proof_stages.label: Greedy shortest-basis algorithm from canonical cycles
proof_stages.summary: For H1 over Z/2, the homology classes form a vector space, so a greedy independent selection from any cycle set containing an optimal basis is optimal; shortest-path-tree canonical cycles over all roots provide such a set, yielding an initial polynomial-time algorithm whose bottleneck is independence testing.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological annotations for fast independence tests
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines simplex annotations as bit vectors whose cycle sums classify homology classes, explains their cohomological meaning, states they can be computed in matrix-multiplication time, and uses them to reduce the shortest-basis complexity substantially.

proof_stages.label: Rips-complex approximation from point samples
proof_stages.summary: For an epsilon-sample of a smooth closed manifold embedded in Euclidean space, a Rips complex at a suitable scale is built and the shortest-basis algorithm is applied, with reach and convexity-radius hypotheses giving length bounds that converge to the true shortest H1 basis as sampling densifies.

proof_stages.label: Integer and linear programming formulation of homologous-chain localization
proof_stages.summary: The talk switches to the optimal homologous cycle/chain problem, notes NP-hardness over Z/2, formulates the Z-coefficient version as an integer linear program using the boundary matrix and sign-splitting, and identifies total unimodularity as the condition allowing relaxation to an ordinary LP.

proof_stages.label: Topological criteria for total unimodularity
proof_stages.summary: Polynomial-time solvability follows when the relevant boundary matrix is totally unimodular, and the speaker gives topological sufficient and equivalent conditions: orientable compact manifolds, a torsion-free relative-homology characterization over pure subcomplexes, and a useful R3/Alexander-duality case.

proof_stages.label: Implementations and open algorithmic directions
proof_stages.summary: The talk shows experimental localizations, discusses practical LP bottlenecks and available shortest-basis code, and closes with questions about improving the asymptotic bounds and supporting efficient updates under changing complexes.
